content.claim: Hungarian research (Horn, Corvinus/TARKI) finds students in 6- and 8-year gimnaziums achieve approximately 10% greater progress than similarly-able and similarly-situated peers in 4-year gimnaziums, but the mechanism is contested: improvement may derive from peer effects, teacher sorting, or genuine pedagogical advantage of the 6+6 structure rather than selection per se.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Demography lens
limitations: Effect size (10%) is reported without confidence intervals or statistical significance thresholds
Panel data observed only from grade 8 onward, missing pre-admission trajectory variation across school types
Isolating causal school effect requires unobserved controls for selection/admission period effects
Self-reported principal motives (pedagogical vs. competitive) are not independently verified
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
